profile.bio: Welsh historian. Gwynedd. occasional YouTube commenter.

posts.text: Interesting treatment of the medieval period, though you're conflating two separate campaigns. Easy mistake if you're relying on secondary sources.
The pronunciation of Llywelyn at 4:20 made me wince, for what it's worth. Two lateral fricatives, not an 'L' followed by 'win'.
I'd recommend cross-referencing with the Brut y Tywysogion — it gives a very different account of those negotiations.
Fair point about Gwynedd's strategic position, though I'd argue the geography mattered more than the politics at that stage.
That map is slightly off — Merioneth wasn't part of the disputed territory at that date.
Shared this with my department. Colleagues were impressed by the production quality even where they disagreed with the framing.

profile.bio: Philosophy PhD. Oxford. occasional YouTube rabbit holes.

posts.text: The argument at 12:30 has a hidden premise — you're assuming the counterfactual is stable, which I'd contest.
I think you're right about the conclusion but for the wrong reasons. The evidence you cite doesn't actually support lemma 3.
Appreciate the pushback in the replies, but nobody has addressed the original objection — they've addressed a weaker version of it.
To be clear I'm not defending the position, I'm pointing out that the refutation doesn't work. There's a difference.
Changed my mind after reading the paper you linked. The third section addresses exactly what I was worried about.
I was wrong in my earlier comment — conflated two distinct claims. Correcting myself publicly since I was fairly emphatic.

profile.bio: Climate science researcher. communicating science to general audiences.

posts.text: It is worth noting that the methodology section understates the uncertainty bounds. Furthermore, the confidence intervals shown in the graph at 8:15 are not correctly propagated.
To shed light on the paleoclimate data: the Holocene optimum is often misrepresented in popular communication. The regional variability is substantial.
In summary, the video is well-produced but conflates two distinct feedback mechanisms. This is a common error in science communication and not a criticism of the creator.
I must correct the claim at 11:30 — the IPCC AR6 figure cited is the central estimate, not the upper bound. These are not interchangeable.
Additionally, it is important to emphasize that model uncertainty and measurement uncertainty require different treatments in risk assessment frameworks.
My collaborators and I are writing a response paper to the study referenced here. I will share it when it clears peer review.
I appreciate the thorough treatment of the carbon budget concept. Moreover, the framing around tipping points is more careful than I usually see in this format.

profile.bio: Daily briefings on culture, business and technology.

posts.text: The shift toward decentralized platforms continues to reshape creator economics. Revenue models are being restructured at every level of the industry.
Retail investors have historically underperformed institutional counterparts. Behavioral finance offers several explanations for this persistent gap.
Supply chain visibility has emerged as a critical competitive differentiator. Organizations that invested early in tracking infrastructure are now realizing the returns.
Remote work adoption rates have stabilized across most sectors. The office occupancy data from major metros shows a consistent 40-50% utilization floor.
Electric vehicle adoption continues to face infrastructure bottlenecks in secondary markets. Charging network density remains the primary constraint.
Generative AI integration into enterprise workflows shows uneven adoption rates. Compliance-heavy industries are lagging by 18-24 months.
Healthcare consolidation trends have accelerated since 2022. Regional hospital systems are being absorbed at a pace not seen since the managed care era.
Open source foundation models are narrowing the performance gap with proprietary alternatives. Enterprise adoption is moving faster than most predictions suggested.
Cross-border payment friction is declining as CBDC pilots expand. Settlement times that once required days are being compressed to seconds.
Talent acquisition costs in the software sector have moderated substantially from 2021 peaks. Compensation benchmarks are resetting across all experience tiers.

profile.bio: Mathematics education researcher. University of Cambridge. occasional commenter.

posts.text: It is worth noting that the pedagogical approach demonstrated here aligns closely with constructivist learning theory. The implications for classroom practice are, moreover, substantial.
In conclusion, whilst the methodology is sound, the generalisation in the final section overstates the available evidence. This is a common challenge in science communication.
Furthermore, the distinction between procedural and conceptual knowledge — which the video touches upon at 8:40 — is fundamentally important to how we design assessments.
In the realm of mathematics education, it is essential to consider the role of prior knowledge. Additionally, the cultural dimension of numeracy is often overlooked in these discussions.
I would recommend cross-referencing this with Vygotsky's zone of proximal development. At its core, the scaffolding question is what the research consistently returns to.
Moreover, my own research suggests that the transfer effect described here is considerably more context-dependent than the presenter implies. I have shared my papers in a reply below.
I've been trying to figure this out for three days. Thank you so much.
the way I completely missed the step at 11:00 the first two times I watched this lol
update: got it working! took me another hour but your video was the key
came back for a different tutorial and realised I already commented here lol. this channel is great
genuinely saved my project with this. appreciate you keeping it free
